title: Transforming the Patient Access Center with AI
gist: BCG argues health systems are misusing AI in patient access centers by treating it purely as a cost/efficiency play, when the larger prize is converting the access function from a cost center into a revenue engine via three parallel strategies—automate, augment, amplify—unlocked only by heavy clinical-operations change management.

themes.theme: Reframing access from cost center to revenue engine

insights.claim: Most health-system executives view AI in patient access centers merely as a way to cut costs and boost efficiency, which overlooks the larger opportunity to reshape access end-to-end into a revenue engine.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: The differentiated value thesis is treating access AI as a growth platform, not a labor-cost tool; systems that stop at efficiency will underperform peers who pursue revenue capture.
insights.quote: This perspective overlooks a significant opportunity to use AI to reshape the access function from end to end—shifting it from a cost center to a revenue engine.
insights.locator: Yet when system leaders consider deploying AI

insights.claim: A substantial share of patient demand is currently lost at the access channel, with up to 40% of scheduling-related interactions unresolved on first contact and many calls abandoned due to long waits, failed transfers, and limited after-hours coverage.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Quantifies the leaked-demand pool AI can recapture; this unresolved volume is the addressable revenue that reframes access economics.
insights.quote: Up to 40% of scheduling-related interactions go unresolved on first contact.

insights.claim: The access center should be treated as a proof case for a health system's broader enterprise AI ambitions, not just a functional upgrade.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Signals where health-system AI budgets and vendor attention will concentrate first; access is the beachhead for enterprise AI spend.
insights.quote: They also see the access center as a “proof case” for their broader enterprise AI ambitions.
insights.locator: Many of our clients are deploying AI

themes.theme: Automate: end-to-end containment of routine workflows

insights.claim: High-frequency, heavily scripted intents—appointment confirmation, scheduling/rescheduling, cancellation, referral and authorization status, and wayfinding—typically represent more than half of total inbound volume at large health systems and are prime targets for full AI containment.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Over 50% of call volume is automatable with no clinical judgment, defining the near-term labor-displacement and containment TAM for access-center AI vendors.
insights.quote: they typically represent more than half of total inbound volume at large health systems

insights.claim: No single vendor currently offers a full end-to-end virtual agent solution for access centers, and no single approach fits every system.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: The market is fragmented and unconsolidated—named players (Hyro, Sierra, Decagon, Avaamo) plus build-in-house are all viable, flagging an early-stage competitive landscape and potential consolidation runway.
insights.quote: No single vendor currently provides a full, end-to-end solution virtual agent, and no single “best” approach will work for every health system

insights.claim: Financial modeling of virtual-agent build-vs-buy decisions must account for uncertain future variables such as AI token cost, alongside speed to value, vendor dependency, and integration with custom business logic.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Token-cost trajectory is an explicit swing factor in access-center ROI models, tying health-system AI economics to broader LLM inference pricing trends.
insights.quote: detailed modeling of financial implications, including potentially uncertain variables like future token cost

insights.claim: About 40% to 50% of access-center calls will still require human interaction in the near term due to complexity, sensitive topics, or consumer preference.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Caps the near-term automation ceiling and defines the durable market for agent-assist tooling rather than full replacement.
insights.quote: about 40% to 50% of calls will still require human interaction in the near-term
insights.locator: While many patient access interactions can be automated

insights.claim: Up to 60% of human agent time is currently spent on tasks that AI could augment, and agent-assist tools can cut average handle time by 30% to 40%.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Concrete productivity deltas (60% augmentable time, 30-40% AHT reduction) that underpin the efficiency half of the ROI case and justify agent-assist procurement.
insights.quote: Agent-assist tools can drive reductions in average handle time (AHT) by 30% to 40%
insights.locator: Among our clients, up to 60% of human agent time

insights.claim: AI can auto-generate post-call summaries, in-basket messages, emails, and downstream tickets, reducing after-call work by up to 2 minutes per interaction.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Quantifies a discrete automation win (up to 2 minutes saved per call) that compounds across millions of interactions into material labor savings.
insights.quote: reducing after-call work by up to 2 minutes per interaction
insights.locator: After a call, AI auto-generates call summaries

insights.claim: Most health systems manually sample fewer than 5% of access-center interactions for QA, leaving the vast majority of agent behavior—including compliance gaps and patient-safety escalations—unobserved.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: The sub-5% review rate exposes a large unmonitored risk and coaching gap that AI-driven QA can close by evaluating far more interactions in real time.
insights.quote: Most health systems sample fewer than 5% of interactions for manual review
insights.locator: One of the most underutilized levers

insights.claim: The same AI QA architecture built to evaluate human agents can also score virtual agents, letting leaders compare quality across automated and human channels.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Cross-channel QA comparability becomes a governance tool for deciding how aggressively to shift volume from human to virtual agents.
insights.quote: the architecture used to automate human-agent QA can be used to evaluate virtual agents

insights.claim: Incorporating growth strategies—demand capture, ancillary attachment, referral management, proactive outreach, and personalized 'segments of one'—raises the projected margin uplift of AI-driven access optimization by 3-5x versus focusing on cost alone.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: The 3-5x margin multiplier is the headline quantified argument for the revenue-engine thesis and the metric to hold vendors and internal programs to.
insights.quote: increases the projected margin uplift of AI-driven access center optimization by 3–5x versus focusing on cost alone
insights.locator: For our clients, incorporating growth-related strategies

insights.claim: AI-driven referral tracking and follow-up reduces network leakage and improves closed-loop visibility, which is especially valuable for high-margin commercial patients and specialty procedures.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Points to where the revenue upside concentrates—retaining high-margin commercial and specialty volume within the network—guiding where systems should aim AI first.
insights.quote: reducing leakage within the network and improving closed-loop visibility for care teams (especially critical for high-margin commercial patients and specialty procedures)
insights.locator: Referral management. AI tracks referral status

insights.claim: Treating each call as a demand signal to convert rather than an interaction to resolve cheaply reframes 24/7 AI coverage and automated follow-up as tools to recapture demand lost to staffing limits and after-hours gaps.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Reframes call handling from cost-minimization to conversion, the mindset shift required to realize the revenue-engine upside.
insights.quote: treating each call not as a low-value interaction to resolve quickly and inexpensively but as a demand signal to convert, a relationship to deepen, and a care gap to close

themes.theme: The critical enabler: clinical operations and change management

insights.claim: Technology, vendors, architecture, and infrastructure represent only about 30% of an AI access transformation, while people and change management account for 70%—the portion most often overlooked and underinvested.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: The 70/30 split warns that AI ROI hinges on organizational change, not tooling—diligence on a system's operational readiness matters more than its vendor choice.
insights.quote: Technology models, vendors, architecture, and infrastructure represent only about 30% of an AI transformation. People and change management account for 70%

insights.claim: AI in access centers will fail unless health systems codify the formal and informal scheduling business logic—often living in shared Excel trackers or unwritten agent knowledge—into structured inputs the technology can consume.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Uncodified, fragmented business logic is the hidden blocker; systems lacking clean, documented scheduling rules will see AI underdeliver regardless of vendor quality.
insights.quote: AI will only be as good as the foundation upon which it is built
insights.locator: Because AI will only be as good

insights.claim: Expanding contact-center agents' booking authority into visit types they are currently prohibited from booking is one of the highest-leverage operational fixes, and one AI alone cannot unlock.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Highlights that much of the upside depends on political/operational permission changes inside practices, not technology—an execution risk investors should probe.
insights.quote: Relaxing these restrictions is one of the highest-leverage operational fixes available, and one that AI alone cannot unlock.

insights.claim: Successful systems treat access-center transformation as a cross-functional strategic program with executive and clinical sponsorship, not as an IT project akin to a vendor migration.
insights.speaker: Natasha Taylor
insights.so_what: Governance model is a leading indicator of which systems will actually capture value—IT-project framing predicts underperformance.
insights.quote: not an IT project akin to a vendor migration
insights.locator: The systems that succeed treat access center transformation
